coredata.dc:title: Hybrid of carbon quantum dots modified g-C3N4 nanosheets and MoS2 nanospheres: Indirectly promote hydroxyl group production for efficient degradation of methyl orange
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Xinxin
Chen, Changzhao
Zang, Jiyuan
coredata.dc:description: Azo dye molecules represented by methyl orange (MO), widely exist in industrial wastewater, and their efficient degradation requires hydroxyl groups with high oxidation activity. g-C3N4/MoS2 heterojunctions are widely used in many photocatalytic applications. However, typical type-II narrow bandgap heterojunctions have holes that cannot be directly oxidized to hydroxyl groups, which is an important barrier to MO degradation. In this work, g-C3N4 nanosheets were modified with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and combined with spherical MoS2 nanoparticles to form CCN/MS composite photocatalysts. CQDs can be used as electronic reservoir to efficiently separate photogenerated carriers and facilitate the indirect generation of hydroxyl radicals. The results showed that the CCN/MS (10:1) sample had the most excellent degradation performance, with 93 % MO degradation in 120 min, while the control sample of CN/MS (10:1) without introducing CQDs had a degradation rate of only 24 % within the same time interval. In the mixed MO/MB degradation experiments, the degradation of MO was preferred to that of MB, suggesting the existence of a competitive mechanism between the two molecules. Our work proposes a possible pathway for the generation of hydroxyl groups using narrow bandgap semiconductor heterojunction catalysts, which provides a new perspective for understanding the degradation mechanism of pollutant molecules in real industrial wastewater.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of CCN/MS composite photocatalyst.

(a) XRD patterns of the pure g-C3N4, MoS2, CQDs modified g-C3N4 (CCN), and CCN/MoS2 composite with different mass ratio; (b) FT-IR spectra of the pure g-C3N4, g-C3N4 with CQDs modification, and a representative composite sample of CCN/MoS2 (10:1).
SEM image of CCN/MS (10:1) (a) and elemental mapping spectra (b-e).
TEM images of MoS2 nanospheres (a), and the composite of CCN/MS (10:1) (c); HRTEM images of MoS2 nanospheres (b), and the composite of CCN/MS (10:1) (d).
XPS spectra of the pure g-C3N4 and CCN/MS (10:1) (a) C 1s, (b) N 1s; XPS spectra of the pure MoS2 and CCN/MS (10:1) (c) Mo 3d, (d) S 2p.

(a) UV–vis spectra of pure g-C3N4, MoS2, CCN, and CCN/MS hybrids with different mass ratio between CCN and MoS2; (b) The reduced absorption curve (absorption at 300 nm as reference) of a series of samples with the inset showing Tacus curves.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of MO and MB under visible light irradiation with different catalysts; (b) The variation of the increment of C/C 0 in CCN/MS samples relative to the control sample of CN/MS under two degradation pollutants of MO and MB with irradiation time. (c) Recyclability test of CCN/MS (10:1) degradation of MO.

The mixed degradation test of the CCN/MS (10:1). (a) Absorption curves of MO-MB dye mixtures with the inset showing the change in solution color over time during the degradation process; (b) The degradation curves of two pollutants of MO and MB, which are separated from the optical absorption curves of mixed degradation. (c) The adsorption performance and the apparent rate constant K of the CCN/MS composite catalyst and the controlled sample of CN/MS for MO and MB dye molecules under visible light before and after mixing.
(a) Transient photocurrent responses, and (b) the EIS of pure g-C3N4, CCN/MS (10:1) and the control sample of CN/MS (10:1).
Mott-Schottky plots of CCN and MoS2 at 1 kHz, 5 kHz and 10 kHz frequencies.
The photocatalytic mechanism of CCN/MS composite for MO degradation.
Photocatalytic degradation efficiency of MO over CCN/MS (10:1) in the blank and in the presence of K2Cr2O7, BQ, EDTA and IPA free radical test for degradation of MO (a) and MB (b).

head.ce:title: Hybrid of carbon quantum dots modified g-C3N4 nanosheets and MoS2 nanospheres: Indirectly promote hydroxyl group production for efficient degradation of methyl orange

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanics and Optoelectronics Physics, Anhui University of Science and Technology, Huainan 232001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanics and Optoelectronics Physics
Anhui University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanics and Optoelectronics Physics, Anhui University of Science and Technology, Huainan 232001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Azo dye molecules represented by methyl orange (MO), widely exist in industrial wastewater, and their efficient degradation requires hydroxyl groups with high oxidation activity. g-C3N4/MoS2 heterojunctions are widely used in many photocatalytic applications. However, typical type-II narrow bandgap heterojunctions have holes that cannot be directly oxidized to hydroxyl groups, which is an important barrier to MO degradation. In this work, g-C3N4 nanosheets were modified with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and combined with spherical MoS2 nanoparticles to form CCN/MS composite photocatalysts. CQDs can be used as electronic reservoir to efficiently separate photogenerated carriers and facilitate the indirect generation of hydroxyl radicals. The results showed that the CCN/MS (10:1) sample had the most excellent degradation performance, with 93 % MO degradation in 120 min, while the control sample of CN/MS (10:1) without introducing CQDs had a degradation rate of only 24 % within the same time interval. In the mixed MO/MB degradation experiments, the degradation of MO was preferred to that of MB, suggesting the existence of a competitive mechanism between the two molecules. Our work proposes a possible pathway for the generation of hydroxyl groups using narrow bandgap semiconductor heterojunction catalysts, which provides a new perspective for understanding the degradation mechanism of pollutant molecules in real industrial wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The industrialization process of high energy consumption has led to increasingly serious water pollution problems in China [1]. Among the 129 basic pollutants announced by the US Environmental Protection Agency, there are 9 categories and a total of 114 are organic pollutants [2]. Among them, azo dye represented by methyl orange (MO) has become one of the most important organic pollutants in the discharge of printing and dyeing wastewater [3,4]. In addition, MO dye wastewater is a compound polluted wastewater that is difficult to treat, and discharging it directly into waters can lead to mutations and cancers, among other things, which can seriously endanger human health [5–7]. The common methods of MO dye wastewater treatment include biological treatment [8], coagulation sedimentation [9], adsorption [10], chemical oxidation [11], etc. At present, the most widely used method is photocatalytic oxidation, which shows great potential in dye wastewater treatment [12]. The photocatalytic oxidation method mainly decomposes the oxidant and promotes the chain reaction of the oxidant to produce highly oxidizing groups or ions, attacking the organic matter in the wastewater [13]. This method is suitable for biologically toxic and difficult to degrade organic pollutants, and can oxidize or couple most difficult to degrade organic compounds [14]. Photocatalytic degradation technology has the advantages of being able to perform at room temperature and pressure, completely destroying organic matter, high oxidation efficiency, fast decomposition speed, and no secondary pollution [15,16]. However, it has high pharmaceutical costs, complex reaction equipment, and high processing costs [17]. Therefore, developing wastewater treatment technology based on semiconductor catalysts, further reducing costs, simplifying devices and processes, and exploring reaction activity are all of great significance for the basic research and practical application of wastewater treatment technology.

g-C3N4 and MoS2 are commonly used in the synthesis of semiconductor catalysts. g-C3N4 is a visible light-responsive catalyst with several outstanding advantages, such as simple structure and preparation, abundant raw material sources, and high stability [18–20]. Semiconductor phase MoS2 (2H-MoS2) is also a potential candidate for active photocatalysts (light harvesting materials). Its advantages as an active photocatalyst include a narrow bandgap (1.2–1.9 eV), a suitable band position, a layered stacking structure, and being an ideal platform for basic research on photocatalysis [21,22]. Due to the high energy band and structure matching as well as good chemical compatibility of g-C3N4 and 2H-MoS2, their effective combination forms a high-quality interface that induces directional carrier transfer, which helps to effectively separate photo-generated carriers, thereby extending their lifespan and improving photon efficiency [23]. Although both semiconductors have a narrow bandgap that ensures their broad light absorption, they are unable to simultaneously increase the redox potential [24]. Besides, the majority of the g-C3N4/MoS2 heterojunctions formed are of type II [25,26], in which electrons (e−) and holes (h+) are clustered in the conduction band (CB) of MoS2 and the valence band (VB) of g-C3N4. Unfortunately, the VB potential of g-C3N4 is not positive enough for h+ to directly oxidize OH− or H2O to OH [27]. The generation of hydroxyl species (OH) is crucial for the degradation of MO [28], so many g-C3N4/MoS2 heterojunctions have weak ability to degrade MO.

In general, there are three ways to form OH in semiconductor catalyst solutions: 1) O
H −
+
h +
→ ⋅ OH ; 2) H2O +
h +
→ ⋅ OH ; 3) e− + O2
→ ⋅O2 −, ⋅
O 2 −
+
H 2
O →
H 2
O 2
→ ⋅ OH . The first two methods are direct h+ oxidation, which requires VB potentials of at least 1.99 eV ( O
H −
/ ⋅ OH ) [29] and 2.7 eV (H2O/ ⋅ OH ) [30] vs. RHE, respectively. The third method generates O2 − and intermediate H2O2 through e− reduction, and then the e− further reduces H2O2 to indirectly generate OH. Since the VB potential of the h+ in the g-C3N4/MoS2 heterojunction is more negative than the O
H −
/ ⋅ OH potential of 1.99 eV, it is virtually impossible to directly oxidize the h+ to produce OH. Therefore, we attempt to generate OH through the third pathway. In the g-C3N4/MoS2 composite, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are used to modify g-C3N4. By utilizing the e− absorption properties of CQDs, e−-h+ pairs separation is achieved, resulting in e− aggregation at the interface [31]. High concentration electron enrichment is beneficial for the formation of O2 − and H2O2, which is an important condition for the indirect generation of OH. In addition, CQDs have unique up-conversion luminescence properties [32], and the light absorption range of g-C3N4/MoS2 modified by CQDs will be significantly enhanced, further increasing the utilization of visible light.

In this study, a step-by-step method was used to regulate g-C3N4 using CQDs, and then composite with MoS2 to form a CQDs modified g-C3N4/MoS2 (CCN/MS) heterojunction. The optimized CCN/MS sample showed a degradation rate of 93.4 % for MO under visible light within 120 min, while the CN/MS control sample without CQDs only degraded about 34.7 % of MO within the same time interval. The degradation of MO benefits from the OH generated by photogenerated e− through a series of reduction reactions, which is inseparable from the function of CQDs as electron reservoirs. More importantly, the mixed degradation experiments of two different dye molecules, i.e., MO and MB, showed that the degradation rate of MO was even higher than that of individual MO degradation, indicating the practicality of the current CCN/MS catalyst in treating actual wastewater with multiple dye molecules present simultaneously. The result of the free radical capture experiment is that the h+ is almost ineffective, while e−, O2 −, and OH have important inhibitory effects on degradation, which is consistent with the idea of indirectly generating OH through photogenerated e−.

Melamine (C3H6N6, 99 %), citric acid (C6H8O7, 99.5 %), sodium molybdate dihydrate (Na2MoO4·2H2O, 99 %), polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP), thiourea (CH4N2S, 99 %), methylene blue (MB), methyl orange (MO), isopropyl alcohol (C3H8O), benzoquinone (C6H4O2), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (C10H16N2O8) were purchased by Shanghai Macklin Chemical Reagent Company. All reagents are of analytical grade and can be used directly during the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs modified g-C3N4 nanosheets (CCN)
ce:section.ce:para: The g-C3N4 modified by CQDs was synthesized by one-step calcination of a mixture of melamine and citric acid, which is an improvement on the method proposed by Di et al. [33]. In detail, 5 g of melamine, 10 mg of citric acid, and 30 mL of deionized water (DI) were poured into a beaker, sealed with plastic wrap at the top. Then sonicated for 2 h to make a homogeneous mixture and obtain a milky white solution. After drying, it was put into a muffle furnace, and the temperature increase rate was set to 5 °C/min, and the temperature was kept warm for 3 h after reaching 550 °C to obtain yellow block g-C3N4 with CQDs modification. Subsequently, the product was ground and dispersed in DI to ultrasonically exfoliate into nanosheets, denoted as CCN.

MoS2 (abbreviated as MS) nanospheres were prepared by the hydrothermal method. Typically, Na2MoO4·2H2O (500 mg), PVP (300 mg), and thiourea (800 mg) were added to 90 mL DI, respectively. After stirring for 3 h, the mixture was hydrothermally treated at 180 °C for 36 h. Finally, the black crystalline powder was obtained after washing with DI and ethanol alternately for several times and drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis composite of CCN nanosheets/MS nanospheres
ce:section.ce:para: The flowchart including all stages for the preparation of CCN/MS composite catalysts is shown in Fig. 1 . The final step of the process is to use ultrasonic stirring to mix CCN and MS to obtain composite photocatalysts with different mass ratios. Firstly, a certain amount of MoS2 nanomaterials was weighed and poured into 20 mL DI, followed by ultrasonic treatment for 0.5 h to form a uniform suspension. Subsequently, 100 mg of CCN nanosheets were added, violently stirred for 6 h, sonicated for 3 h, and dried to obtain the final product. The addition amounts of MoS2 are 20 mg, 10 mg, and 5 mg, respectively, and the composites are denoted as CCN/MS (5:1), CCN/MS (10:1), and CCN/MS (20:1) according to the mass ratio of CCN and MS. In addition, we synthesized a g-C3N4/MoS2 heterojunction with a mass ratio of 10:1 without CQDs modification using the above method in order to highlight the role of carbon quantum dots. This control sample was named CN/MS (10:1).

The catalysts were subjected to physical phase analysis and crystallinity determination by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku) of Cu-Kα radiation at a wavelength of 0.15406 nm. The crystal surface structure and lattice stripe spacing of the samples were measured by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM-2100HR). HRTEM operates at 200 kV and uses Digital Micrograph software to analyze and distinguish lattice fringes. The surface of the material is analyzed qualitatively, quantitatively and structurally by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi) by means of information on the energy, angle and intensity of the electrons excited from the surface of the material. XPSPEAK41 software was used to analyze sample spectra, and peak shifts caused by any apparent charging were calibrated using the occasional carbon C1s peak at 284.6 eV. The functional groups and molecular structures in the catalyst were obtained by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet 6700) with the samples evenly dispersed in the KBr matrix during measurement. Measurement of ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra (UV–Vis) using a Shimadzu UV2600 device is used to analyze, determine and deduce the composition, content and structure of substances, using BaSO4 powder as a reference. In a dark and closed environment, the amplitude parameter was set to 10 mV and the frequency response range was 0.1 kHz–100 kHz. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was measured under this condition. The frequencies are set to 1 kHz, 5 kHz and 10 kHz to plot the Mott-Schottky (MS) plot at an amplitude of 10 mV.

The difference in degradation of MO and MB dye molecules by the synthesized catalysts was analyzed by loading a filter with a cut-off wavelength of 400 nm on a xenon lamp with a power of 300 W. The concentration of MO/MB solution was controlled to be 10 mg/l (100 mL) and the amount of catalyst injected was 20 mg. In order to ensure the accuracy of the experiments, the suspension containing the MO (MB) contaminant and the photocatalyst needs to be placed under dark conditions and stirred for 60 min in advance to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The solution was placed at a distance of about 20 cm directly under the xenon lamp with magnetic stirring, and after an interval 4 mL of supernatant was drawn up into a centrifuge tube using a rubber-tipped buret. The centrifuged supernatant was transferred to a cuvette and its absorption spectrum was measured. The photocatalytic degradation performance of MO/MB mixed solutions was carried out under the same experimental conditions.

The XRD patterns of pure g-C3N4, MoS2, CCN/MS composites with different composite ratios, and the reference sample CN/MS were comparatively analyzed (Fig. 2(a)). Two distinct peaks are present in the as-prepared g-C3N4, located at 12.9° and 27.5°, respectively, the former belonging to the interlayer stacking crystal plane (002) of the graphitic carbon nitride conjugate aromatic system and the latter to the weak in-plane repeating structure (100) crystal plane of the tris-triazine ring unit [34]. Weak diffraction peaks with 2θ of about 12.1°, 32.4°, and 57.6° are, respectively, attributed to the diffraction of crystal planes (002), (100), and (110) in the MoS2 hexagonal phase (JCPDS No.37–1492) [35]. The introduction of CQDs results in a weakening of the diffraction peak of g-C3N4 (002), and when combined with MoS2, this weakening effect will be further enhanced, indicating a significant suppression of the tris-triazine ring unit due to the interference of foreign components. It is difficult to observe MoS2 lattice plane reflection in CCN/MS nanocomposites, possibly due to low MoS2 content or poor crystallization that cannot be detected [36].

The presence or change of functional groups or chemical bonds in pure g-C3N4, CQDs modified g-C3N4, and a representative composite sample of CCN/MS (20:1) are investigated by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2(b)). The infrared absorption peaks belonging to the g-C3N4 structural unit are clearly visible in the logarithmic coordinate system on the horizontal axis. The broad peak vibrations of OH (3430 cm−1) and NH (3292 cm−1) bonds originate from the adsorption of water molecules and uncondensed amino groups on the g-C3N4 surface [37]. A series of absorption peaks with locations at 1640, 1564, 1461, 1405, 1329, and 1239 cm−1 are mainly originated from the stretching vibrations of CN heterocycles in the g-C3N4 framework [38]. A clear sharp peak is observed near 804 cm−1, which is caused by the triazine unit in g-C3N4 [39]. The absorption peak structure of CCN is basically the same as that of pure g-C3N4, but slightly weakened, which suggests that CQDs have a negative impact, but the basic skeleton remains unchanged. In contrast, the peak intensities of the CCN/MS composite were significantly decreased or even disappeared compared to those of g-C3N4, indicating that the interface coupling attenuated the vibration of the g-group in g-C3N4, which may be a sign of successful synthesis of CCN/MS heterojunctions.

The morphology, crystalline quality, and interface coupling of the synthetics were obtained by SEM and (HR) TEM analysis. MoS2 nanospheres with a diameter of about 200 nm are in close contact with the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheets (Fig. 3(a)), and no obvious CQDs are observed in the SEM images due to the low content of CQDs and the small diameter of the particles (about a few nanometers). From the elemental mapping images, it is seen that the elements C, Mo, S, and N were uniformly distributed (Fig. 3(b–e)). Pure MoS2 exhibits nano-spherical shape with an average diameter of approximately 200 nm from TEM observations as displayed by SEM, formed by the self-assembly of numerous MoS2 nanosheets (Fig. 4(a)). The 0.64 nm lattice spacing (Fig. 4(b)) in the HRTEM of MoS2 can be attributed to its (002) lattice plane [40]. The g-C3N4 nanosheets and MoS2 nanospheres are clearly shown in the TEM image of the CCN/MS (10:1) heterostructure (Fig. 4(c)). It is worth mentioning that thin nanosheets are more conducive to expanding the exposure of more active sites than blocky g-C3N4. The close interface contacts between g-C3N4 and MoS2 nanospheres are characterized by clear boundary lines in the HRTEM images of CCN/MS (10:1) (Fig. 4(d)). Moreover, some CQDs with a size of 5 nm or so embedded in the g-C3N4 matrix can be well recognized, and the 0.25 nm lattice stripe displayed in the local enlarged image in the lower right corner is precisely the (101) crystal plane spacing of CQDs [41]. The TEM results indicate that MoS2 nanospheres are supported or surrounded by thin layers of g-C3N4, and their interface contacts are close, which may be more favorable for photogenerated e−-h+ transfer at the interface.

The element species, chemical states, and interfacial coupling of the CCN/MS composite were analyzed using XPS spectroscopy. Of these, only two peaks are required for the C 1s spectral fit of pure g-C3N4 (Fig. 5(a)), the peak at 284.6 eV belongs to the CC in the amorphous species on the surface of the sample, and the other one is the sp2-bonded C of N–C=N in g-C3N4 (288 eV) [42,43]. The difference is that the C 1s spectrum of CCN/MS (10:1) has an additional fitted peak (286.1 eV) of CO or hydroxyl from the CQDs [44]. It is noteworthy that since the peak does not exist in pure g-C3N4, the area ratio of the CC bond to N-C=N increases from 0.11 to 0.49 with the addition of CQDs, suggesting that the source of the peak is most likely the hydroxyl group in the CQDs. The N 1 s spectrum can be decomposed into 3 peaks (Fig. 5(b)) belonging to C-N=C (398.5 eV), N-(C)3 (399.4 eV), and the foreign NH edge groups (401 eV) [45,46]. As for the Mo 3d spectrum ((Fig. 5(c))), two sets of peak structures were observed by fitting. Among them, the characteristic peaks of 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 of Mo4+ correspond to 228.6 and 231.7 eV [47,48], while the other set of Mo 3d peaks at 232.8 and 235.8 eV are considered to be a small amount of Mo6+ ions oxidized by air [49]. The S 2p spectra of both MoS2 and CCN/MS (10:1) show two characteristic peaks at 161.5 (S 2p3/2) and 162.9 eV (S 2p1/2), respectively (Fig. 5(d)), indicating the existence of S2− [50].

UV–vis spectroscopy was used to examine the samples' absorption capacities (Fig. 6(a)). Compared to the 450 nm absorption edge of pure g-C3N4, MoS2 and CCN exhibit stronger light absorption, indicating that the introduction of CQDs and MoS2 is favorable for making full use of sunlight. Under the dual absorption enhancement assisted by CQDs and MoS2, the absorption of visible light by CCN/MS composite samples exceeded that of CQDs modified g-C3N4 (CCN) and binary composite (CN/MS (10:1)), with a significant elevation in the absorption step in the wavelength range of 450–700 nm. Moreover, CQDs also exhibit significant infrared activity due to their up-conversion luminescence (Fig. 6(b)). The reduced absorption curve (absorption at 300 nm as reference) shows that the absorption of CCN and CCN/MS composite samples in the near-infrared region (shaded area) is significantly better than that of the reference sample CN/MS (10:1), indicating that the up-conversion of CQDs plays an excellent role [51]. The band gaps (E g) of pure g-C3N4 and CCN/MS (10:1) are 2.74 eV and 2.38 eV, according to the Tauc diagram (the inset of Fig. 6(b)). It is obvious that the absorption edge of the CCN/MS composite undergoes a red shift of 0.36 eV relative to g-C3N4, which may be due to the dual effects of the modulation of the g-C3N4 energy level by CQDs and the interfacial interaction between g-C3N4 and MoS2.

Fig. 7(a)–(c) shows the curves of various photocatalysts prepared for the degradation of MO pollutants, and MB is also selected as the target pollutant as it carries ions of opposite polarities to MO. It is expected that the comparative study of the two will reveal the characteristics of the catalyst adsorption process. Pure MoS2 and g-C3N4 exhibit poor photocatalytic degradation of MO, with C/C 0 only decreasing to about 80 % after 120 min of irradiation (without deducting adsorption effects) due to rapid charge carrier recombination (Fig. 7(a)). The combination of g-C3N4 and MoS2 slightly improved the catalytic activity of the binary composite CN/MS (10:1), reducing the C/C 0 to about 65 % within 120 min. As compared with the controlled sample of CN/MS (10:1), the introduction of CQDs in the CCN/MS series composite catalysts results in a significant improvement in MO degradation performance, with CCN/MS (10:1) exhibiting the most excellent catalytic activity. In sharp contrast, the degradation effect on MB molecules of the CCN/MS catalysts is not significant in spite of considerably increased adsorption. The relationship between the amount of change in C/C 0 of CCN/MS relative to the controlled sample of CN/MS and the degradation time is shown in Fig. 7(b), which clearly demonstrates the stronger ability of the currently prepared CCN/MS catalyst to degrade MO. As for the optimal catalyst of CCN/MS (10:1), the C/C 0 increment of MO degradation is close to 50 %, while the value of MB degradation is only about 20 % within 100 min. This significant difference can be attributed to the complexity of the structure of the different dye molecules themselves, the ionic charge, and the associated degradation mechanism [52,53]. However, the current CCN/MS catalyst can effectively degrade MO, indicating that the introduction of CQDs plays a key role. To test the stability of the catalyst, three cycles of MO degradation experiments were conducted on CCN/MS (10:1) under simulated visible light (Fig. 7(c)). The photocatalysts were collected by centrifugation (8500 rpm/min, 7 min), washed several times alternately with deionized water and ethanol, and subsequently dried in an oven at 60 °C. The dried photocatalysts do not require further treatment and can be used directly in the next cycle experiment. The degradation rate of MO decreases from 93.4 % in the first cycle to 85.8 % in the third cycle. A small decrease in photocatalytic activity after multiple cycles is reasonable and common due to the loss of the photocatalyst. This result indicates that the catalytic activity of CCN/MS remains after three cycles.

In order to reveal the reason behind the difference in the degradation performance of MO and MB dyes, we conducted a mixed degradation experiment of the two dyes, which also meets the practical requirements of industrial wastewater treatment as the wastewater generally contains a variety of organic pollutant molecules. As shown in the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 8(a)), the MO/MB mixed solution is degraded by CCN/MS (10:1), and as the reaction time progressed, the color of the solution gradually became lighter, and the characteristic peaks of the pollutants (MO: 468 nm [54]; MB: 665 nm [55]) had been weakening, which indicated that the pollutants' concentration is decreasing gradually. It is notable that the absorption peak of MO almost disappears at 40 min, while the absorption peak of MB only significantly weakens beyond this time. Undoubtedly, MO is preferentially degraded when MO/MB are mixed. Moreover, the presence of MB also accelerates the degradation of MO at its own expense. As shown in Fig. 8(b), when the CCN/MS (10:1) sample is mixed for degradation, about 80 % of MO is degraded (excluding adsorption) after 40 min of light exposure, while MO alone is only 42 % (Fig. 7(a)). In contrast, for MB, its degradation in the MO/MB mixture is significantly inhibited, with the degradation rate decreasing from 61 % (only MB) to 48 % (MO/MB mixture) after 100 min of light exposure, indicating the complex interactions among MO, MB, and catalyst in the present mixed solution [56]. Fig. 8(c) summarizes the adsorption performance and the apparent rate constant K of the CCN/MS composite catalyst and the controlled sample of CN/MS for MO and MB dye molecules under visible light before and after mixing, from which the correlation between adsorption and degradation may be obtained. Except for CCN/MS (5:1), the adsorption rates of both pollutants significantly increase after dye mixing. The adsorption rates of MO and MB in CCN/MS (10:1) samples increase from 8.8 % and 35.8 % before mixing to 23.1 % and 51.7 % after mixing, respectively. In terms of degradation, the kinetic constant of MO degradation significantly increases after mixing, while the K value of MB decreases rapidly, which coincides with the degradation curve in Fig. 8(b). Based on the data analysis of adsorption and degradation before and after mixing, the improvement in MO degradation performance of the CCN/MS catalyst after mixing may be related to the increase in dye adsorption. It is well known that the MB dye molecules are generally positively charged and easily attracted to the surface of negatively charged CCN/MS catalysts [57], resulting in high adsorption rates. However, negatively charged MO molecules have extremely low adsorption rates due to their electrostatic repulsion with the catalyst [58]. After the two dye molecules are mixed, the increase in MO adsorption rate may be due to the neutralization or shielding effect of MB on the surface charge of the catalyst. Due to the fact that the adsorption sites may also serve as catalytic active centers, the azo dye molecules of MO are preferentially attacked by highly oxidizing hydroxyl groups after adsorption [53], and the degradation of the thioazobenzene dyes of MB occurs almost after the end of MO degradation [52].

The efficient degradation of MO depends on OH, and the characteristic of CQDs capturing e− plays a crucial role in promoting OH generation, which will inevitably facilitate the efficient separation of photogenerated e−-h+ pairs. For this purpose, transient photocurrent response and EIS spectroscopy are conducted to evaluate the photogenerated carrier separation and transfer performance of CCN/MS composite catalysts. The photocurrent density of the CCN/MS (10:1) composite is approximately twice as high as that of g-C3N4 and 1.7 times higher than that of the reference sample of CN/MS (10:1) (Fig. 9(a)), indicating that the introduction of CQDs can effectively separate photo-generated carriers. The minimum arc radius (Fig. 9(b)) of the CCN/MS (10:1) sample shown in the EIS Nyquist plot shows that it has the lowest charge transfer resistance. Furthermore, the EIS Nyquist plot diagrams of g-C3N4, CCN/MS (10:1) and the control sample of CN/MS (10:1) are fitted by ZsimpWin software. The equivalent circuit model (the inset in Fig. 9(b)) includes five parameters listed in Table 1 , i.e., R s, R ct, R a, C, and Q, which are subject to the solution resistance, interfacial charge transfer resistance, electrolytic resistance, space charge capacitance, and electrochemical double-layer capacitance, respectively [59–61]. The interface transfer resistance of CCN/MS (10:1) is 22.4 kΩ, which is approximately one fourth of CN/MS (10:1) and one fiftieth of g-C3N4, indicating that the formation of heterogeneous interfaces significantly reduces R ct, which will be further reduced after adding CQDs.

The in-depth analysis of degradation mechanisms requires obtaining information on energy level positions, charge transfer directions, and the generation, evolution, and mechanism of action of free radicals under light. Fig. 10 shows the MS curves of CCN and MoS2 samples at different frequencies, which can be used to determine the flat band potentials (V fb) of each semiconductor component. V fb generally takes the intercept of the MS curve converging to the same point on the horizontal axis at different testing frequencies and is determined as −1.12 and − 0.05 V for CCN and MoS2, respectively. The positive slopes of the MS curves of CCN and MoS2 indicate that both semiconductors are n-type, indicating that their conduction band potential (V CB) is approximately V fb [62]. Since the bandgap of CCN is known to be 2.46 eV, its valence band potential (V VB) can be calculated as 1.34 V based on the equation
E VB
=
E CB
−
E g
, where E VB, E CB, and E g represent the VB edge potential energy, CB edge potential energy, and bandgap width, respectively. Due to the lack of an obvious absorption edge in the absorption curve of MoS2, its E g cannot be determined using the Tauc method. The
E VB
and
E g
of MoS2 can be predicted by Mulliken's electronegativity theoretical equation [63]:
E VB
= X −
E e
+ 0.5
E g
and
E CB
=
E VB
−
E g
, where X and
E e
are semiconductor electronegativity and standard hydrogen electrode (about 4.5 eV). Due to the X value of MoS2 being 5.33 eV [64], the
E VB
and
E g
are estimated for MoS2 to be 1.71 and 1.76 eV, respectively, which is consistent with previous literature reports [65,66].

Based on the positions of energy levels discussed above, a CCN/MS heterojunction energy level diagram can be drawn, and possible photocatalytic mechanisms for MO degradation, including carrier transfer and photodegradation processes, are proposed. The current CCN/MS heterojunction exhibits a typical type II structure (Fig. 11 ). Under visible light irradiation, photogenerated e−-h+ pairs are induced in the CB and VB of CCN and MoS2, respectively. Photo-induced e- will transfer from the CB of CCN to the CB of MoS2, while photo-induced h+ will migrate from the VB of MoS2 to the VB of CCN, resulting in effective separation of photogenerated carriers and a reduced complexation rate. The h+ are clustered in the VB of CCN, and its potential of 1.34 eV is not enough to oxidize OH− or H2O to OH. Fortunately, the e− transferred to the CB of MoS2 are absorbed by CQDs, which further increases the separation efficiency of e−-h+ pairs. The surviving e− will indirectly generate OH through a series of reactions as follows: (1)
O 2
+ 2
H +
+ 2
e −
→
H 2
O 2
; (2)
H 2
O 2
+
e −
→ O
H −
+ ∙ OH , which become important active species degrading MO.

In addition, CQDs can also convert absorbed infrared light into visible or even ultraviolet light with a short wavelength through up-conversion luminescence, which will enhance the utilization of radiated light. Therefore, the introduction of CQDs in CCN/MS catalysts has improved the catalytic performance of the system from at least two perspectives: enhanced absorption and favorable OH generation, directly leading to a significant improvement in the system's ability to degrade MO compared to CN/MS reference samples. To further validate the generation pathway of OH and their crucial role in MO degradation, the photodegradation MO removal rate of CCN/MS (10:1) with different sacrificial agents as probes is tested within 120 min. During the experiments, IPA (5 mL), BQ (5 mg), K2Cr2O7 (5 mL), and EDTA (5 mL) are used to sacrifice OH, ·O2 −, e−, and h+ [67]. The addition of IPA will maximize the inhibition of MO degradation (Fig. 12(a)), resulting in a degradation rate of 15 % within 60 min, which is consistent with the fact that OH is the most important active free radical. The introduction of BQ and K2Cr2O7 also significantly inhibits the degradation of MO, as sacrificing e− and ·O2 − can become obstacles to OH generation, which confirms the indirect pathway of OH generation in the solution. In contrast, the consumption of h+ does not significantly affect the degradation rate of MO, further confirming that the path of direct oxidation of OH through h+ is not feasible. As shown in Fig. 12(b), e− hardly plays a role in MB degradation, and the effect of ·O2 − is also weak. Both h+ and OH can play a certain role in MB degradation, which can well explain that the addition of CQDs can only slightly enhance MB degradation.

In this work, CQDs modified g-C3N4/MoS2 heterojunctions (CCN/MS) were prepared using a stepwise method. By exploiting the electron-absorbing property of CQDs, the electrons are aggregated at the interface, and the high concentration of electron aggregation facilitates the formation of O2 − and H2O2, which further promotes the indirect generation of OH, the main active substance for MO degradation. This pathway compensates for the fact that in Type II heterojunctions, the holes are not sufficient to oxidize OH− or H2O directly to OH. The optimized CCN/MS (10:1) sample showed a degradation efficiency of 93.4 % of MO in 120 min under visible light, whereas the control sample CN/MS showed only 34.7 % degradation of MO in the same time. Free radical trapping experiments confirmed that e−, O2 − and OH are the vital active substances in MO degradation, whereas h+ hardly plays a role, which is in keeping with the idea that OH is produced indirectly through photogenerated electrons. More importantly, the mixed degradation experiments of two different dye molecules, MO and MB, showed that the degradation rate of MO was even higher than that of single MO, which demonstrated the practicality of the present CCN/MS catalysts in treating real wastewater with the simultaneous presence of multiple dye molecules.

Xinxin Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Writing – original draft. Changzhao Chen: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Jiyuan Zang: Investigation, Data curation.
